quiz.title: Calculus Limits Quiz 17
quiz.subtitle: Intermediate Value Theorem — AP Applications (20 Questions)
quiz.subject: Calculus

questions.question: For the Intermediate Value Theorem to apply to f on [a,b], which must be true?
questions.explanation: IVT requires f to be continuous on the closed interval [a,b]. Differentiability is not required.

questions.question: If f is continuous on [1,5] with f(1)=-3 and f(5)=7, IVT guarantees a root exists because:
options: f(1) and f(5) have opposite signs
questions.explanation: f(1)<0 and f(5)>0, so by IVT there exists c in (1,5) with f(c)=0.

questions.question: If f is continuous on [0,2] with f(0)=1 and f(2)=9, which value is guaranteed?
questions.explanation: IVT guarantees all values between f(0)=1 and f(2)=9, so 5 is guaranteed.

questions.question: For f(x)=x²+1 on [0,3], does IVT guarantee a root?
questions.explanation: f(0)=1>0, f(3)=10>0, same sign. IVT doesn't guarantee a root when endpoints have the same sign.

questions.question: f(x) = {x+2 if x<3, kx-1 if x≥3}. For continuity at x=3, find k.
questions.explanation: Left: 3+2=5, Right: 3k-1. Set equal: 5=3k-1 → k=2.

questions.question: f is continuous on [-2,2] with f(-2)=-5, f(0)=3, f(2)=-4. Minimum number of roots?
questions.explanation: f(-2)<0, f(0)>0 → root in (-2,0). f(0)>0, f(2)<0 → root in (0,2). At least 2 roots.

questions.question: f, g continuous on [0,1] with f(0)=1, f(1)=3, g(0)=2, g(1)=0. How do we prove f(c)=g(c) for some c?
questions.explanation: Let h(x)=f(x)-g(x). h(0)=1-2=-1<0, h(1)=3-0=3>0. IVT on h gives h(c)=0, so f(c)=g(c).

questions.question: Why doesn't IVT apply to f(x)=1/x on [-1,1]?
options: f is not defined at x=0
f is not continuous on the interval
questions.explanation: f is undefined at x=0, which means f is not continuous on [-1,1]. IVT requires continuity.

questions.question: p(x)=x³-x-1 has a root in which interval?
Prove cos(x)=x has a solution in [0,1].
questions.explanation: Let f(x)=cos(x)-x. f(0)=1-0=1>0, f(1)=cos(1)-1≈0.54-1<0. IVT gives root.

questions.question: e^x=3-x has a solution in which interval?
IVT guarantees a solution exists. Does it guarantee uniqueness?
questions.explanation: IVT only guarantees existence of at least one solution, not uniqueness. Multiple solutions may exist.

questions.question: f continuous on [0,1] with 0≤f(x)≤1. Prove f(c)=c for some c.
questions.explanation: Let g(x)=f(x)-x. g(0)=f(0)-0≥0, g(1)=f(1)-1≤0. IVT on g gives g(c)=0, so f(c)=c.

questions.question: For IVT to guarantee a root, what must be true about f(a) and f(b)?
questions.explanation: For a root to be guaranteed, f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs (one positive, one negative).

questions.question: f(x) = {x² if x<2, ax+1 if x≥2}. For continuity at x=2, find a.
questions.explanation: Left: 2²=4, Right: 2a+1. Set equal: 4=2a+1 → a=3/2.

questions.question: f continuous on [1,4] with f(1)=2, f(4)=10. Is f(c)=7 guaranteed?
questions.explanation: Yes, 7 is between f(1)=2 and f(4)=10. IVT guarantees f(c)=7 for some c in (1,4).

questions.question: ln(x)=x-2 has a solution in which interval?
Which is NOT required for IVT?
questions.explanation: Differentiability is NOT required for IVT. Only continuity on a closed interval is needed.

questions.question: f continuous on [0,6] with f(0)=-1, f(2)=3, f(4)=-2, f(6)=4. Minimum roots?
sin(x)=x/2 has how many solutions in [0,π]?
questions.explanation: Let f(x)=sin(x)-x/2. f(0)=0 (one solution). f(π/2)=1-π/4>0, f(π)=0-π/2<0. Another root in (π/2,π). At least 2 solutions.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the Intermediate Value Theorem.
scoreMessages.70: Very good! You understand IVT conditions and applications.
scoreMessages.50: Good progress! Review when IVT applies and how to use it.
scoreMessages.0: Focus on: continuity + closed interval + sign change = root exists.
